policy_statement: San Francisco International Airport (SFO) will evaluate an all-electric Terminal Central Utility Plant to cut Scope 1 airport emissions ~80% by 2030 and prioritize all-electric equipment replacements across campus buildings (BO.2-6).
mechanism_description: SFO will evaluate an efficient all-electric terminal central utility plant and prioritize all-electric equipment replacements across campus buildings to reduce direct (Scope 1) airport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building end-use equipment electrification → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ direct (Scope 1) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Replace or avoid fossil-fueled building equipment (boilers, furnaces, water heaters, CHP, terminal utility plant components) by installing all-electric systems (heat pumps, electric boilers, electric resistance where necessary) and associated electrical upgrades.  
(2) Mediating process: Eliminates/avoids combustion of natural gas/diesel on-site, reducing CO₂ (and often methane leakage associated with fuel use) and other combustion byproducts; remaining emissions shift to the electricity supply, which can be reduced via cleaner grids and/or contracted renewable electricity.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions—especially Scope 1—leading to mitigation benefits; co-benefits can include improved local air quality.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing direct on-site fossil fuel combustion in buildings, which lowers GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Electrifying building equipment primarily reduces on-site fossil fuel combustion, cutting direct GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although electrification can also improve energy efficiency (Resource Efficiency) and can aid resilience depending on design (Adaptation), the core, mechanism-defining effect is emissions mitigation through combustion reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is highly carbon-intensive, near-term net reductions could be smaller (or in rare cases negative), but the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant effect remains Scope 1 combustion reduction; pairing with renewable electricity procurement strengthens mitigation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that electrifies building/central-plant equipment to replace fossil combustion shares the same primary mitigation pathway—reducing direct combustion emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., efficiency-led electrification, renewable supply coupling, resilience co-benefits) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge case where electrification shifts emissions to a very high-carbon grid (reducing net mitigation benefit), or where the primary stated purpose is resilience/backup power rather than emissions; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly targets direct fossil combustion reduction, supporting Mitigation as primary.
key_indicators: "evaluate an efficient, all-electric Terminal Central Utility Plant"; "reduce total direct (Scope 1) airport emissions by approximately 80% by 2030"; "prioritize all-electric equipment replacements throughout campus buildings"
co_benefits: Improved local air quality from reduced on-site combustion; potential operational efficiency/maintenance benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a planning/evaluation element, but the core intent is implementation of electrified plant/equipment replacements (capital retrofit), so classified as infrastructure investment rather than planning-strategy.
